coredata.dc:title: Visualized adsorption and enhanced photocatalytic removal of Cr6+ by carbon dots-incorporated fluorescent nanocellulose aerogels
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hanyu
Chen, Yehong
Mo, Meiqing
Dorsel, Padonou-Kengue Patrick
Wu, Chaojun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) and titanate nanofibers (TNs) were mixed with TEMPO-oxidized nanocellulose (TOCNC) to prepare fluorescent nanocellulose aerogels (FNAs) by a Schiff base reaction. The resulting FNA can detect the adsorption of Cr6+ through the fluorescence quenching in CDs and promote the removal of Cr6+ through the synergistic effect of CDs in photocatalysis. The optimized FNA has a maximum adsorption capacity of 543.38 mg/g, higher than most reported Cr6+ adsorbents. This excellent performance is due to the porous structure of the aerogel, which gives it a high specific surface area of 20.53 m2/g and provides abundant adsorption sites. Simultaneously, CDs can enhance the amino-induced Cr6+ adsorption, improve the photocatalytic performance of TNs, and expose more adsorption sites through electrostatic adsorption of amino-induced reduction products (Cr3+). This study explores the preparation of visualized nanosorbents with enhanced photocatalytic removal of Cr6+ and provides a new direction for nanoscale photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Photocatalysis
Heavy metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of adsorption properties of FNA-4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The National Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2023MC130 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation flow chart of FNA. (b) Fluorescence intensities of FNAs with different CDs loadings (the CDs amounts gradually increased from left to right).

(a) TEM image of CDs; (b) UV–vis spectrum of CDs; (c) Emission spectra of CDs excited at different wavelengths; (d) Excitation spectra of CDs at different emission wavelengths; (e) Emission spectra of CDs in the presence of different metal ions; (f) FTIR spectra of CDs; (g) Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of different Cr6+ concentrations (from 0.0001 mg/l to 100 mg/l from left to right).

Characterizations of FNA-4. (a) SEM image; (b)-(c) EDS spectra; (d) XPS spectra; (e) XRD pattern; (f) FTIR spectrum; (g) thermogravigram; (h) micro-CT image; (i) laser confocal microscopic image; (j) fluorescence changes in FNA-4 induced by different heavy metal ions; (k) The left is the quenching effect of nine ionic mixed solutions except Cr6+ on FNA-4, and the right is the quenching effect of ten ions on FNA-4; (l) Competitive adsorption in mixed solutions of different ions; (m) Competitive adsorption time diagram of three ions (pH = 2, 298 K, 30 mg,)

(a) Effect of pH value on the adsorption of Cr6+ by dark treatment and photocatalysis (298 K, 1 h, 30 mg); (b) Effect of adsorption time on the adsorption of Cr6+ by dark treatment and photocatalysis (298 K, 200 min, 30 mg, pH = 2); (c) Effect of dark treatment and photocatalysis on the adsorption of Cr6+ with different amounts of adsorbent (298 K, 200 min, 10, 30, 50, 70, 90, 120 mg, pH = 2); (d) Effects of different amounts of adsorbent on the adsorption of Cr6+ by dark treatment and photocatalysis (298, 313, 328 K, 200 min, 30 mg, pH = 2); (e) Frendlich fitting curves at different temperatures under photocatalysis (30 mg, pH = 2, 298 K, 200 min); (f) Langmuir fitting curves at different temperatures under photocatalysis (30 mg, pH = 2, 298 K, 200 min).

(a) Pseudo-first order kinetic equation for photocatalytic reduction reaction (30 mg, pH = 2, 298 K, 200 min); (b) Pseudo-second-order kinetic equation for photocatalytic reduction (30 mg, pH = 2, 298 K, 200 min); (c) Adsorption and photocatalytic reusability of FNA-4.

Adsorption mechanism of FNA-4. (a) FTIR spectra of FNA-4 before and after Cr6+ adsorption; (b) XPS total survey spectra; (c) Cr2p spectra; (d) N1s spectra; (e) O1s spectra; (f) Ti2p spectra of FNA-4 before and after Cr6+ adsorption.

Analysis of photocatalytic mechanism. (a) UV–vis DRS spectra and band gaps of TNs and FNA-4 (illustration); (b) VB XPS valence spectrum of TNs and FNA-4; (c) Photocurrent response of TNs and FNA-4; (d) EIS Nyquist plots of TNs and FNA-4; (e) Adsorption and photocatalytic activity of FNA-4 under different inhibitor conditions; (f) EPR spin-trapping spectra.
TNs and FNA-4 reaction processes at different Cr concentrations.
The equilibrium isotherm model parameters of Cr6+ were determined by FNA-4 under photocatalysis.
Comparison of Cr6+ adsorption properties between FNA-4 and previous adsorbents.

row.entry: Chitosan supramolecularly cross linked with trimesic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption kinetics parameters of Cr6+ by FNA-4 under photocatalysis.

head.ce:title: Visualized adsorption and enhanced photocatalytic removal of Cr6+ by carbon dots-incorporated fluorescent nanocellulose aerogels

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking,Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, Shandong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking,Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, Shandong, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon dots (CDs) and titanate nanofibers (TNs) were mixed with TEMPO-oxidized nanocellulose (TOCNC) to prepare fluorescent nanocellulose aerogels (FNAs) by a Schiff base reaction. The resulting FNA can detect the adsorption of Cr6+ through the fluorescence quenching in CDs and promote the removal of Cr6+ through the synergistic effect of CDs in photocatalysis. The optimized FNA has a maximum adsorption capacity of 543.38 mg/g, higher than most reported Cr6+ adsorbents. This excellent performance is due to the porous structure of the aerogel, which gives it a high specific surface area of 20.53 m2/g and provides abundant adsorption sites. Simultaneously, CDs can enhance the amino-induced Cr6+ adsorption, improve the photocatalytic performance of TNs, and expose more adsorption sites through electrostatic adsorption of amino-induced reduction products (Cr3+). This study explores the preparation of visualized nanosorbents with enhanced photocatalytic removal of Cr6+ and provides a new direction for nanoscale photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the rapid development of modern industry has negatively impacted living environments, and heavy metal pollution has become an increasing concern [1]. Cr6+ released from industrial wastewater can produce antimicrobial bacteria, posing a severe risk to the water ecosystem. In addition, Cr6+ may cause skin ulcers, respiratory congestion, and liver swelling in the human body and has a strong teratogenic effect [2,3]. To remove Cr6+ from wastewater, adsorption [4,5], photocatalysis [6] and membrane filtration [7] have been widely used for water treatment. However, the preparation of adsorbents with high removal efficiency is challenging. Therefore, designing an adsorbent that can be easily synthesized for effective Cr6+ adsorption is necessary [8].

Cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) are highly crystalline nanomaterials derived from natural celluloses. In addition to their sustainability, renewability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, high rigidity, and large specific surface area, CNCs contain many hydroxyl groups on the surface, enabling various surface modifications [9–12]. One of the most common practices for CNC preparation is through the oxidization of 2,2,6,6-tetrmethylpyridine-1-oxy (TEMPO) to obtain TEMPO-oxidized CNC (TOCNC). The carboxyl groups on the surface of the TOCNC are favorable for removing heavy metal ions through electrostatic adsorption and ion exchange [13].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based material widely used as probes for heavy metal ions owing to their biocompatibility, strong fluorescence emission, small size, and high-temperature stability [14–16]. Simultaneously, the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl functional groups on the surface of CDs make them water-soluble and easily functionalized. Their electrostatic attraction and chemical complexation with heavy metal ions in water can quench the fluorescence of CDs. Moreover, CDs show the potential to improve photocatalytic activity [17]. Therefore, introducing CDs into adsorbents can have multiple advantages [18,19]. Luo et al. synthesized fluorescent magnetic chitosan hydrogels (FMCHs) by incorporating amino-functionalized Fe3O4 nanoparticles (AF-Fe3O4 NPs) and CDs-modified cellulose nanofibers (CNFs) through a crosslinking method. The maximum adsorption capacity of FMCH for Cr6+ was 212.1 mg/g [18]. Peng et al. crosslinked CDs, titanate nanofibers (TNs), and cellulose nanocrystals (CNs) to produce a fluorescent nanocellulose-based hydrogel (FNH). The maximum adsorption capacity of FNH for Cr6+ was 648.4 mg/g [20]. Zhang et al. prepared a novel nanocellulose/carbon gel (NCH) using CNs, CDs, and zinc oxide (ZnO)/silver bromide (AgBr) nanocomposites. The CDs enhanced the adsorption of hexavalent Cr6+ onto the amino groups in the NCH, and the photocatalytic activity of the ZnO/AgBr nanocomposites was improved via photoelectron transfer. However, there are few such studies that can detect both adsorption and photocatalytic coordination.

In this study, nitrogen-doped CDs and TNs were prepared using a one-pot method. They were then mixed with TOCNC and crosslinked with aldehyde-based bacterial cellulose (A-BC) using a Schiff base reaction. After freeze-drying, the fluorescent nanocellulose composite defined as aerogel (FNA) according to [21,22] was prepared for the adsorption of Cr6+. The adsorption mechanism was analyzed using the adsorption kinetic characteristics, adsorption isotherm model, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) peak fitting, and the reusability and photocatalysis on the adsorption of the FNA were further studied.

TOCNC, with a carboxyl content of 1.8 mmol/g, was purchased from Tianjin Wood Spirit. Polyethylene imine (PEI; Mw = 70,000, Macklin), titanium sulfate (AR grade, Macklin), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH; AR grade, Aladdin) were used in the synthesis. A Cr6+ standard solution (1000 g/mL) and the solvent H2O were purchased from the National Nonferrous Metal Electronic Materials Analysis and Testing Center GB (Beijing) Inspection and Certification Co., Ltd., respectively.

Nitrogen-doped CDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. First, TOCNC and PEI were mixed at a 1:1 ratio in 200 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring. The mixture was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave (250·mL) lined with Teflon and heated at 200 °C for 48 h. After cooling to room temperature, the supernatant was centrifuged and dialyzed for three days to obtain the CDs.

TNs with two structural formulas (Na2Ti3O7 and Na1.5H0.5Ti3O7) were prepared using a reported method [23]. A titanyl sulfate solution (30 g, 12 wt%) and a sodium hydroxide solution (67 mL, 15 M) were mixed. The mixture was then transferred to a high-pressure reactor lined with Teflon and heated at 200 °C for 48 h before cooling to room temperature. The products were neutralized with deionized water and freeze-dried to obtain TNs powder.

First, 1 g of BC and 4 g of NaIO4 were stirred and allowed to react for 12 h in the dark to obtain A-BC, which was then purified by dialysis. The aldehyde group content was determined as 0.53 mmol/g by hydroxylamine hydrochloride titration.

Then, 1 g of TOCNC, 0.1 g of TNs, and 20 mL of 25 % PEI solution were mixed and stirred for 2 h. Varying amounts of CDs (0.015, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, 0.125, 0.15 g) were added and stirred for 1 h. Subsequently, 0.2 g of A-BC was introduced for crosslinking to obtain a fluorescent nanocellulose hydrogel. The hydrogel was purified by dialysis to remove unreacted substances and then freeze-dried to obtain FNAs (FNA-1, FNA-2, FNA-3, FNA-4, FNA-5, FNA-6, and FNA-7 corresponding to the various amounts of CDs). The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1(a). The fluorescence intensities of FNA-1 to FNA-7 are shown in Fig. 1(b). With the increasing quantities of CDs, the fluorescence of FNA increased first and then decreased (Fig. S1(a)). FNA-4 had the highest fluorescence intensity and was used for subsequent adsorption experiments.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the surface morphologies on a Hitachi Regulus-8220 system. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan) and steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS-1000, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK) were used to detect the fluorescence characteristics of the samples. The characteristic peaks of the functional groups were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). An ultraviolet-visible near-infrared fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000) was used to perform ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). The crystal structures of the samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD; Nikaku Smartlab SE, Rigaku, Japan). A simultaneous thermal analyzer (STA449 F3, Netzsch-Gerätebau GmbH, Germany) was used to determine the thermal stability of the samples.

The adsorption of Cr6+ on FNA-4 was measured in the dark in a batch experiment. The FNA-4 was thoroughly mixed with a Cr6+ solution in a 50-mL conical flask. The flask was then transferred to a constant-temperature oscillation chamber, and the solution was collected at different time points to determine the adsorption kinetics. Adsorption was performed at different concentrations and temperatures for 200 min (a constant time for subsequent experiments), and the solution was collected to determine the adsorption isotherm. In the Cr6+ adsorption experiment, the concentration of Cr6+ was determined on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (723 pc, Huanghua Faithful Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai) at 540 nm using the 1,5-diphenylcarbazide method. The total Cr concentration was determined on an atomic absorption spectrophotometer (GGX660, Beijing). Each experiment was repeated twice, and the average of the three measurements was reported.

The experiments were conducted in a photochemical reaction chamber. A 500-W high-pressure mercury lamp was used as the light source. The pH of the solution was adjusted by adding 0.1 mol/l HCl or NaOH (the volume was negligible). First, 50 mL of 50 mg/l Cr6+ and 30 mg FNA-4 were thoroughly mixed and allowed to adsorb in the dark for 200 min. The light was then turned on, and the photocatalytic was run for 200 min. Every 20 min, 1 ml of the reaction mixture was collected and filtered. The concentration of Cr6+ was determined at 540 nm on a UV-VIS spectrophotometer using the instrument and method stated in Section 2.6.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the spherical CDs are uniformly distributed, and their average diameter is 5.722 nm. The absorption bands of the CDs are centered at 223 nm and 325 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 2(b)). Fig. 2(c) shows the emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, wherein slight excitation-dependent emission is observed, and the optimal excitation wavelength is 360 nm. Because many oxygen-containing functional groups have local energy levels at the surface, the emission behavior of CDs is based on excitation. Fig. 2(d) shows the excitation spectra of CDs at different emission wavelengths, and the optimal emission wavelength for CDs is 390 nm. Fig. 2(e) shows the influence of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. When CDs contact Cr6+, their fluorescence intensity is almost zero, indicating that CDs are efficiently quenched by Cr6+. The FTIR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2(f)) shows two characteristic peaks at 3340 and 1641 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of the NH group, respectively. This proves that PEI is successfully doped with the CDs. Fig. 2(g) shows the fluorescence quenching of the CDs with different concentrations of Cr6+. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreases gradually with the increasing Cr6+ concentration. It almost disappears at 60 mg/l, indicating that the CDs can be used to detect Cr6+ visually within a certain concentration range. Meanwhile, we tested the effect of the Cr6+ concentration range on the fluorescence intensity of CDs (Fig. S1(b)), and the results showed that with the increase of Cr6+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased, indicating a good linear relationship between the two.

The SEM image of TOCNC in Fig. S1(c) shows its smooth and uniform surface, while the surface of TNs has some slender strips (Fig. S1(d)). The surface of FNA-4 is rough with a uniform and dense pore structure and long fibers, indicating that CDs and TNs are successfully combined with TOCNC (Fig. 3(a)). Figs. S2(a) and (b) show the N2 adsorption–desorption curves of TOCNC and FNA-4. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area (SBET) of TOCNC is 5.18 m2/g, while the SBET of FNA-4 is 20.53 m2/g, which is attributed to the construction of a 3D porous structure [24].

The EDS spectrum of FNA-4 shows that its surface contains N and Ti (Figs. 3(b) and (c)). The chemical binding states of the samples were determined by XPS, and the FNA-4 contains C, O, N, and Ti, indicating that the chemical binding states of FNA-4 are good (Fig. 3(d)).

The XRD patterns of TOCNC, TNs, and FNA-4 are shown in Fig. 3(e). The TOCNC pattern exhibits three peaks at 15.2°, 16.1°, 22.7°, and 34.5°, corresponding to the (1 0 1), (1 0 1), (0 0 2), and (0 0 4) crystal planes, respectively. The eight diffraction peaks of TNs are found at 10.7° ((1 0 0)), 25.2° ((2 0 1)), 29.9° ((0 0 3)), 34.4° ((0 0 2)), 36.4° ((3 0 1)), 38.9° ((0 1 3), 48.6° ((0 2 0), and 52.9° ((4 1 1)). The XRD pattern of FNA-4 contains the characteristic peaks of TNs and TOCNC, confirming the successful loading of the TNs and TOCNC onto FNA-4.

Fig. 3. (f) shows the FTIR spectra of the TOCNC, TNs, and FNA-4. Owing to the stretching vibration of CO, a strong characteristic peak at 1058 cm−1 is detected in the spectra of TOCNC and FNA-4 [25]. Simultaneously, a new peak related to the stretching of –CO–NH– emerges at 1654 cm−1 for FNA-4, indicating the binding of CDs and PEI to the TOCNC [26]. TNs and FNA-4 exhibit a characteristic peak at 911 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of TiO [27]. The FTIR spectrum of A-BC (Fig. S2 (c)) shows a stretching vibration peak of CO at 1737 cm−1 [22], indicating that BC has been successfully modified.

Fig. 3. (g) shows the thermograms of the TOCNC, TNs, and FNA-4. The weights of the three materials decrease slightly over the 30–100 °C range. In the 100–200 °C range, the weight loss in TOCNC is due to the evaporation of water molecules between layers and the crystallization within the material [28]. In the 200–300 °C range, the thermal decomposition of TOCNC produces gas and volatiles, resulting in rapid weight loss. The maximum weight loss is 55 % at 335 °C, and only 27.5 % of the original mass remains at 800 °C. TNs show excellent thermal stability, with a residual mass of 91.9 % at 800 °C. The residual mass of FNA-4 is 46.7 % at 430 °C and 43.6 % at 800 °C, demonstrating that the thermal stability of TOCNC is improved after the loading of TNs.

The structural characteristics of FNA-4 were observed using micro-CT (Fig. 3(h)). Consistent with the SEM images, the fluorescent aerogel has a crosslinked three-dimensional structure, which can provide multiple active sites for metal ions to contact the functional groups on FNA-4. The fiber structure of FNA-4 is visible under a laser confocal microscope (Fig. 3(i)).

Previously, we demonstrated that CDs can be used as fluorescent probes for Cr6+. Therefore, selective quenching characterization of the prepared FNA-4 were conducted using different metal ions (K+, Ni+, Cd2+, Sn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Cr6+, and Mn2+) at the same concentration (1000 mg/l). As shown in Fig. 3(j), the quenching effect of Cr6+ on FNA-4 is the most distinct, whereas the other metal ions induce insignificant changes in fluorescence. To exclude the influence of other ions on the detection of Cr6+, we also tested the quenching effect of nine ion-mixed solutions except Cr6+ on FNA-4 (Fig. 3(k), left) and ten ions on FNA-4 (Fig. 3(k), right). The results showed that FNA-4 did not affect the detection of Cr6+ in ion mixtures. This may be because Cr6+ has the strongest electron-trapping capability; as a result, more electrons can be transferred from CD to Cr6+, resulting in the strongest fluorescence quenching signal [29]. In addition, we also selected two other ions (Fe3+ and Hg2+) that have a great influence on the fluorescence of FNA-4 and configured them with Cr6+ into a mixed solution (150 mg/l, 50 mg/l separately for three ions) to test their competitive adsorbability. The results are shown in Fig. 3(l) and (m). It can be seen that FNA-4 has a prominent removal rate of Cr6+. This is attributed to the attraction of the amino group of FNA-4 to Cr6+. Therefore, FNA-4 can not only be used as a solid phase probe for Cr6+, but also as a selective adsorbent for Cr6+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of adsorption properties of FNA-4
ce:section.ce:para: In the adsorption experiment, FNA-4 and Cr6+ solutions were mixed in the dark for 200 min to reach adsorption equilibrium, followed by photocatalytic reduction reactions.

As shown in Fig. 4(a), the adsorption capacity of Cr6+ increases and then decreases with the increase in pH for both the dark treatment and photocatalysis. The adsorption capacity (Qe) reaches its maximum at pH = 2. When the pH is 1, the amino group is deprotonated, and the H2CrO4 is mainly uncharged, resulting in a low Qe. At pH = 2–6, chromium ions are predominantly present as HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2−, and the electrostatic attraction between the chromium ions and FNA-4 increases the Qe. At pH = 2, the adsorption capacity for Cr6+ is further reduced by deprotonation. The results showed that the optimum pH for Cr6+ adsorption by FNA-4 is 2 [30,31].

Fig. 4(b) shows the effect of the adsorption time on Cr6+ under dark conditions and during photocatalysis. The adsorption equilibrium is achieved after dark treatment for 180 min, and the removal rate of Cr6+ is only 74 %. In contrast, Cr6+ can be completely removed after 160 min of photocatalysis.

Fig. 4(c) shows the effect of different amounts of adsorbent under dark treatment and photocatalysis. The adsorption of Cr6+ under dark conditions is significantly lower than that under photocatalysis. With increased FNA-4 dosage, all Cr6+ ions can be absorbed. However, Cr6+ is completely removed by 30 mg of FNA-4 under photocatalytic conditions, whereas 70 mg of FNA-4 is required in the dark. Therefore, the optimal adsorbent dose is 30 mg.

Fig. 4(d) shows the effects of different temperatures and concentrations of Cr6+ on the adsorption. With an increase in temperature, the adsorption capacity of FNA-4 for Cr6+ increases; therefore, a high temperature is conducive to adsorption. Under dark treatment conditions, the adsorption performance does not improve significantly with increased concentration because the adsorption has saturated.

Fig. 4(e) and (f) show the adsorption isotherm models of FNA-4, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table 1 . The correlation coefficient (R2) values of the Langmuir model at 298, 313, and 328 K are 0.9963, 0.9964, and 0.9998, respectively (Fig. 4(f)). These values were higher than those of the Freundlich model (0.9939, 0.9922, and 0.9856, respectively) (Fig. 4(e)). The adsorption isotherm model under dark conditions is shown in Fig. S3, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table S1. The R2 values of the Freundlich adsorption isotherm model (0.9727, 0.9801, and 0.9094, respectively) are lower than those of the Langmuir adsorption isotherm model (0.9963, 0.9964, and 0.9998, respectively). Therefore, the adsorption of Cr6+ by FNA-4 follows Langmuir adsorption (monolayer adsorption) under dark and photocatalytic conditions. According to the Langmuir model parameters, the maximum dark treatment and photocatalysis adsorption capacities are 111.11 and 543.48 mg/g, respectively. In addition, because 1/n is between 0 and 1, Cr6+ easily reacts with FNA-4.

The adsorption performance of FNA-4 was compared with other sorbents for Cr6+ adsorption. As shown in Table 2 , FNA-4 demonstrates higher adsorption capacity for Cr6+ than most reported adsorbents. The porous structure of FNA-4 provides abundant transport channels and adsorption sites for ion adsorption, and the addition of TNs increases the synergistic effect of photocatalysis to reduce Cr6+. In addition, the simple preparation of FNA-4 offers additional advantages for its practical applications.

The adsorption kinetics of FNA-4 were studied for Cr6+ adsorption; the results are shown in Figs. 5 and S4, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Tables 3 and S2. Fig. 5(a) and (b) show the pseudo-first- and pseudo-second-order models of FNA-4 under photocatalytic conditions, respectively. The R2 of the pseudo-second-level model (0.9557) is higher than that of the pseudo-first-level model (R2 = 0.8653). The R2 value (0.9557) of the pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics model of FNA-4 under dark conditions is higher than that of the pseudo-first-order adsorption kinetics model (R2 = 0.8653). This indicates that the pseudo-second-order model better describes the adsorption kinetics of the FNA under dark and photocatalytic conditions. This also indicates that the removal of Cr6+ mainly occurs through chemisorption.

The reusability of the FNA-4 was investigated by cyclic adsorption experiments. The FNA-4, after the first adsorption (30 mg), was immersed in 100 mL of 0.2 M sodium hydroxide solution for 1 h and then washed to neutral with deionized water. The re-generated FNA-4 was then placed into 50 mL of 50 mg/l Cr6+ solution to continue the adsorption experiment, and the Cr6+ removal rate was measured. After recovering the adsorbent, 5 ml 1 mol/l NaOH was added to soak for 20 min for desorption recombination, and then FNA-4 was dried at room temperature for Cr6+ experiment, as shown in Fig. 5(c). After four cycles of adsorption, the removal rate of Cr6+ by FNA-4 is still greater than 90 %, indicating that FNA-4 has good reusability. However, the gradual decrease in the removal rate of Cr6+ by FNA-4 may be due to partial structural damage caused by the reversible Schiff base reaction or the diffusion of CDs into the adsorption medium.

Fig. 6(a) shows the FTIR spectra of FNA-4 before and after adsorption of Cr6+. The peak at 1580 cm−1 corresponds to the carboxylate ion [38]. The peak at 3343 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH [39]. The intensities of these peaks decrease after adsorption, indicating that the amino and carboxyl groups play key roles in the adsorption of Cr6+ [38,40].

Fig. 6(b) shows the XPS profiles of FNA-4 before and after adsorption. After the adsorption of Cr6+, a Cr peak at 577 eV is observed, indicating that FNA-4 can successfully adsorb Cr. Fig. 6(c) shows the Cr2p spectrum of FNA-4 after the adsorption of Cr6+. The peaks at 589 and 577 eV correspond to the binding energies of Cr2p1/2 and Cr2p3/2, respectively. The peak at 589 eV was deconvoluted into two peaks at 589.5 eV for the Cr6+ peak and 586.3 eV for Cr3+. The peak at 579 eV also consists of two components, i.e., 577.8 eV (Cr6+) and 575.5 eV (Cr3+) [41]. When a portion of Cr6+ is reduced to Cr3+, the hydroxyl group of FNA-4 is oxidized, followed by “adsorption-coupled reduction” [38]. Fig. 6(d) shows the N1s XPS spectra before and after adsorption. Three peaks are observed at 398.8 eV (–N=), 400.7 eV (−NH2), and 401.9 eV (–NH3 +) before adsorption. However, no new peaks emerged after adsorption, indicating that the protonated amino group was electrostatically attracted to Cr6+ rather than complexation. In Fig. 6(e), the fitting of O1s XPS spectra before adsorption shows two peaks at 530.5 eV and 532.1 eV corresponding to CO and CO of FNA-4, respectively. After adsorption, the binding energies of these two peaks decrease slightly, indicating the existence of an ionic bond between –COO− and Cr3+ [42]. The Ti2p spectrum of FNA-4 (Fig. 6(f)) shows two peaks at 463.18 and 463.3 eV. No new peaks appear after the adsorption of Cr6+. This result shows that the reduced Cr3+ is generated by ion exchange with the TNs.

The mechanism of the photocatalytic reduction was studied. UV–vis DRS was first performed to evaluate the optical properties of the TNs and FNA-4 (Fig. 7(a)). Both samples exhibit good UV responses, and the UV absorption capacity of FNA-4 is enhanced by the introduction of CDs [43]. The bandgap energy (Eg) was calculated using the Kubelka-Munk function (Fig. 7(a)). The Eg value of FNA-4 (3.0 eV) is lower than that of TNs, indicating its excellent light absorption properties [44].

Fig. 7(b) shows the valence band (VB) XPS spectra of the TNs and FNA-4. The VB potentials of TNs and FNA-4 are 2.51 eV and 2.04 eV, respectively. Based on the Eg and VB potentials, the conduction band (CB) potentials of the TNs and FNA-4 are −0.65 eV and − 0.96 eV, respectively.

The photocurrent response of TNs and FNA-4 are shown in Fig. 7(c), wherein FNA-4 has a higher saturation photocurrent density owing to its three-dimensional porous structure. The introduction of CDs is also conducive to the separation and migration of photogenerated charge carriers [45].
Fig. 7(d) shows the Nyquist plots obtained by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). FNA-4 has the smallest arc radius, corresponding to the highest charge-transfer rate [46].

Four inhibitors, i.e., p-Benzoquinone (BQ), AgNO3, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and anhydrous ethanol, were introduced as trapping agents for superoxide anions (O2), electrons (e−), hydroxyl radicals (-OH), and holes (h+), respectively [47]. As shown in Fig. 7(e), AgNO3 and BQ have a significant influence on the adsorption and photocatalysis of FNA-4, suggesting that e− and ·O2 − are the main active substances involved in photocatalytic reduction. In addition, 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-N-oxide (DMPO) was used for EPR spin capture to confirm the function of the free radicals [48]. A signal of DMPO-·O2 − is observed under UV irradiation with a stable trailing peak (Fig. 7(f)) [49]. Hence, CDs can act as electron acceptors [50] to facilitate the production of e− and·O2 −, which is one of the main reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of FNA-4 (Fig. 8 .

To confirm the removal mechanism of Cr6+, Cr species were detected in the reaction (Fig. 9 ). During the adsorption, no Cr3+ is found in the solution of the TNs; however, Cr3+ formation is observed in the solution of FNA-4. This indicates that there is an “adsorption-coupled reduction” of FNA-4 during adsorption, which is mainly attributed to the presence of TOCNC. In the photocatalytic process, the concentration of Cr3+ increases with increasing ultraviolet irradiation time. Considering the adsorption capability of FNA-4 is significantly higher than that of TNs, the adsorbed Cr6+ on FNA-4 can be photo-reduced to Cr3+, leading to a high photocatalytic ability of FNA-4.

The mechanism of CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalysis is shown in Fig. 9. First, during the initial adsorption of FNA-4, the negatively charged Cr6+ is attracted by the electrostatic force of the amino-modified CDs [51]. Under ultraviolet light, the photogenerated electrons in the TNs are excited. A large number of photogenerated electrons are rapidly transferred to the CDs surface [52]. The Cr6+ adsorbed on the CDs is then reduced to Cr3+. At the same time, some hydroxyl groups on the TOCNC are oxidized. Owing to the electrostatic repulsion between the amino group and Cr3+, Cr3+ leaves the CDs surface, allowing more active sites for further adsorption and photocatalysis.

In this study, a nanocellulose fluorescent aerogel was prepared, and an excellent synergistic photocatalytic effect was obtained by doping the aerogel with TNs. CDs not only respond to different concentrations of Cr6+ through fluorescence quenching but also enhance the adsorption and photocatalytic synergistic effect of FNA-4 on Cr6+. A series of adsorption and photocatalytic experiments confirmed that the porous structure of FNA-4 and the loading of amino-modified CDs facilitated the adsorption and photocatalysis. FNA-4 exhibited selective fluorescence quenching for Cr6+ for visualization of the adsorption. The maximum adsorption capacity of FNA-4 was 543.48 mg/g under the photocatalytic reaction. This excellent performance is attributed to the CDs-enhanced adsorption-photocatalytic mechanism. This study employed CDs as fluorescence probes for visualized adsorption and as nanoscale photocatalysts to enhance the photocatalytic adsorption synergy, providing a new direction for the development of nanoscale photocatalytic adsorbents.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The National Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2023MC130).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.127206.
